displayTitle: Two Numbers, and the One Extra Neutron

beats.narration: Every atom you'll inspect is pinned down by just two numbers. Get them straight, and isotopes and sources fall into place.

payload.title: NUMBERING THE ATOM
payload.sub: Two numbers tell you what it is — and whether it's hot.

beats.narration: The atomic number Z counts protons — that's the element's identity. The mass number A counts protons plus neutrons. Subtract: A minus Z gives the neutrons.
Fix the protons, change the neutrons, and you get isotopes — same element, different mass. Hydrogen comes three ways; natural iridium blends 191 and 193.
Add one neutron to stable cobalt-59 in a reactor and you get cobalt-60 — same element, now unstable. An unstable isotope is a radioisotope.
So keep it sharp: every element has isotopes, and most are stable. Only the unstable ones decay. One neutron is the whole difference.

payload.sub: Same chemistry — one unstable nucleus does all the work.
